symbols: twin towers — aspiration heavenward and the call to worship
arched doorways — entrance into sacred space and the threshold of the holy
stone construction — permanence and endurance of the faith
cross-topped spires — the centrality of Christ's sacrifice in Christian worship
description: A photograph of a large Romanesque church facade with two tall, pointed towers flanking a central section. The building is constructed of grey-brown stone with multiple tiers of arched windows. The central portion features a triangular pediment above a round-arched entrance. The towers rise to Gothic-influenced pointed spires. The sky is overcast and grey, and modern vehicles are parked at the base, providing scale for the massive structure.
theological_description: The abbey church, as a house of worship dedicated to the glory of God, embodies the scriptural imperative to gather for corporate praise and prayer. Its Romanesque solidity echoes the Psalmist's declaration that God is 'a mighty fortress' (Psalm 46:1), while its upward-reaching towers express the human longing for transcendence and communion with the divine. Sacred architecture throughout the Christian tradition has served as a tangible expression of the heavenly Jerusalem — 'a city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God' (Hebrews 11:10). The church building itself becomes a symbol of the ecclesia, the gathered body of believers built as living stones into a spiritual house (1 Peter 2:5).

scripture_refs.reason: The fortress-like solidity of the abbey expresses God as a mighty refuge and strength
The enduring stone construction points to the city with foundations whose architect is God
The church built of hewn stones symbolizes believers as living stones in God's spiritual house

_meta.title: Abbey of Saint Étienne, Caen, France
